questions.text: Tetany is considered to be the result of a severe degree of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hyperglycemia refers to high levels of glucose (sugar) in the blood. It is commonly associated with diabetes and can lead to various complications over time. However, it is not directly linked to tetany.

Hypercalcemia refers to high levels of calcium in the blood. It can be caused by various factors such as excessive intake of calcium, certain medical conditions, or medications. Tetany is not typically associated with hypercalcemia.
Hypoglycemia is a condition characterized by low blood sugar levels. It can occur in individuals with diabetes who take insulin or certain medications, but it is not typically associated with tetany.

Hypocalcemia is a condition characterized by low levels of calcium in the blood. It can occur due to various factors, such as vitamin D deficiency, certain medical conditions affecting the parathyroid glands, or certain medications. Tetany is often associated with hypocalcemia because low calcium levels can cause muscle spasms and contractions, leading to the characteristic symptoms of tetany.

questions.explanation: Option A: Hyperglycemia refers to high levels of glucose (sugar) in the blood. It is commonly associated with diabetes and can lead to various complications over time. However, it is not directly linked to tetany. Option B: Hypercalcemia refers to high levels of calcium in the blood. It can be caused by various factors such as excessive intake of calcium, certain medical conditions, or medications. Tetany is not typically associated with hypercalcemia. Option C: Hypoglycemia is a condition characterized by low blood sugar levels. It can occur in individuals with diabetes who take insulin or certain medications, but it is not typically associated with tetany. Option D: Hypocalcemia is a condition characterized by low levels of calcium in the blood. It can occur due to various factors, such as vitamin D deficiency, certain medical conditions affecting the parathyroid glands, or certain medications. Tetany is often associated with hypocalcemia because low calcium levels can cause muscle spasms and contractions, leading to the characteristic symptoms of tetany.

questions.text: All of the following are inflammatory arthritis except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic inflammatory autoimmune disease that primarily affects the joints. It occurs when the body's immune system mistakenly attacks the synovium (the lining of the membranes surrounding joints), leading to inflammation, pain, and joint damage.

Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease characterized by the breakdown of cartilage in the joints. It is the most common form of arthritis and is typically associated with aging, wear and tear on the joints, and mechanical factors. Unlike inflammatory arthritis, osteoarthritis does not involve autoimmune or systemic inflammation.

Gouty arthritis, also known as gout, is a type of arthritis caused by the deposition of uric acid crystals in the joints and soft tissues. This leads to inflammation and severe pain, most commonly affecting the big toe. It is considered an inflammatory arthritis because it involves acute attacks of joint inflammation triggered by the accumulation of uric acid crystals.

Psoriatic arthritis is a long-term inflammatory arthritis that occurs in people affected by the autoimmune disease psoriasis. The classic feature of psoriatic arthritis is swelling of the entire fingers and toes with a sausage-like appearance.

questions.explanation: Osteoarthritis. This is the most common form of arthritis and is considered a non-inflammatory type of arthritis.

questions.text: A person has a mutation that produces abnormal sarcoplasmic reticulum. Which of the following will not be a consequence of this mutation?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The myosin heads will not bind the actin filaments
options.explanation: If the sarcoplasmic reticulum is abnormal and does not release calcium ions properly, the myosin heads will not be able to bind to the actin Filaments, which would result in impaired muscle contraction.
Troponin is a protein complex that binds to calcium ions and is responsible for initiating the movement of the tropomyosin molecule, which allows myosin to bind to actin.

options.text: Tropomyosin will remain bound to actin
options.explanation: Tropomyosin is a protein that covers the myosin binding sites on actin in the absence of calcium ions. When calcium ions are released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, they bind to Troponin, which causes Tropomyosin to move and expose the myosin binding sites on actin.

options.text: Calcium ions will not be released into a synapse
options.explanation: The calcium ions required for muscle contraction are released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, while those involved in the transmission of an impulse across a synapse are released via vesicles in the presynaptic neuron.

questions.explanation: The calcium ions required for muscle contraction are released from the SR, while those involved in the transmission of an impulse across a synapse are released via vesicles in the presynaptic neuron.

questions.text: All of the following are types of inflammatory arthritis except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic autoimmune disease that causes inflammation of the joints. It is the most common type of inflammatory arthritis.
Osteoarthritis is a degenerative joint disease that is caused by the breakdown of cartilage in the joints. It is the most common type of arthritis overall.

Gouty arthritis is a type of arthritis that is caused by the buildup of uric acid crystals in the joints. It is a type of inflammatory arthritis, but it is not as common as rheumatoid arthritis or osteoarthritis.

The correct answer is osteomyelitis. Osteomyelitis is an infection of the bone, not an inflammatory arthritis. It can be caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi. It is usually treated with antibiotics, but in some cases, surgery may be necessary. The other three conditions, rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and gouty arthritis, are all inflammatory arthritides.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is osteomyelitis. Osteomyelitis is an infection of the bone, not an inflammatory arthritis. It can be caused by bacteria, viruses, or fungi. It is usually treated with antibiotics, but in some cases, surgery may be necessary. The other three conditions, rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and gouty arthritis, are all inflammatory arthritides.

questions.text: If lactic acid builds up in thigh muscles, it causes muscle tiredness and pain. This condition is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is the correct answer. The condition described, where lactic acid builds up in thigh muscles, causing tiredness and pain, is known as muscle fatigue. Muscle fatigue occurs when muscles are unable to contract efficiently and sustain their performance due to various factors such as the depletion of energy stores, accumulation of metabolic byproducts like lactic acid, or inadequate oxygen supply.
Tetany refers to a condition characterized by prolonged and involuntary muscle contractions, often due to imbalances in calcium and magnesium levels.
Cramps are sudden, involuntary muscle contractions that can cause pain and discomfort.
Oxygen debt in muscle refers to the amount of oxygen required by the muscles to restore normal metabolic function after intense exercise.

questions.explanation: Muscle Fatigue: This option is the correct answer. The condition described, where lactic acid builds up in thigh muscles, causing tiredness and pain, is known as muscle fatigue. Muscle fatigue occurs when muscles are unable to contract efficiently and sustain their performance due to various factors such as the depletion of energy stores, accumulation of metabolic byproducts like lactic acid, or inadequate oxygen supply.

questions.text: A muscle condition resulting from the accumulation of lactic acid and ionic imbalance is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tetany is caused by low blood calcium levels, not lactic acid accumulation or ionic imbalance.
As the muscles keep working constantly, they run out of oxygen, which leads to them respiring anaerobically, which in turn causes lactic acid buildup, thus causing the muscles to fatigue.
Cramps are sudden, involuntary muscle contractions, usually due to overuse or dehydration, not primarily lactic acid accumulation.
Tetanus is a bacterial infection affecting the nervous system, not related to lactic acid buildup or ionic imbalance.

questions.explanation: As the muscles keep working constantly, they run out of oxygen, which leads to them respiring anaerobically, which in turn causes lactic acid buildup, thus causing the muscles to fatigue.

questions.text: Which of the following does NOT occur during skeletal muscle contraction?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ATP is hydrolyzed into ADP and inorganic phosphate (Pi) by the enzyme ATPase. The energy released during ATP hydrolysis changes the angle of the myosin head into a “cocked” position, ready to bind to actin if the sites are available. ADP and Pi remain attached; myosin is in its high-energy configuration.
Calcium binds to troponin to remove tropomyosin from the binding site. It doesn’t attach directly to "myosin".
This option is incorrect because A DOES occur.
This option is incorrect because B DOES NOT occur.

questions.explanation: Calcium binds to troponin to remove tropomyosin from the binding site. It doesn’t attach directly to "myosin".

questions.text: Skeletal muscles are _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct. Skeletal muscles are voluntary. This means that we can consciously control their movement. For example, we can choose to lift our arm or walk.

This option is not correct. Involuntary muscles are not under our conscious control. They work automatically, without us having to think about them. For example, our heart is an involuntary muscle that pumps blood around our body.

options.text: Both Options A and B are correct
options.explanation: Both options cannot be correct. Only option A is correct

options.text: None of these options is correct
options.explanation: This option is not correct, as option A is correct.

questions.explanation: Skeletal muscles comprise 30 to 40% of your total body mass. They're the muscles that connect to your bones and allow you to perform various movements and functions. Skeletal muscles are voluntary, meaning you control how and when they work.

questions.text: What is true about a skeletal muscle cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It has a light and dark band
options.explanation: Skeletal muscles or striated muscles show alternate light and dark regions under a microscope. Hence, A is the correct option.
Skeletal muscles are attached to and cover the bony skeleton. Skeletal muscle fibers are multinucleated and are under voluntary control.
Skeletal muscles are attached to and cover the bony skeleton. Skeletal muscle fibers are multinucleated and are under voluntary control.

options.text: None of these options are correct
options.explanation: Skeletal muscles are attached to and cover the bony skeleton. Skeletal muscle fibers are multinucleated and are under voluntary control.

questions.explanation: Skeletal muscles or striated muscles show alternate light and dark regions under a microscope. Hence, A is the correct option. Skeletal muscles are attached to and cover the bony skeleton. Skeletal muscle fibers are multinucleated and are under voluntary control. Hence, B, C, and D are incorrect options.

questions.text: The muscle that moves a body part away from the midline of the body is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A is wrong because these muscles decrease the angle between the joints.
B is wrong because these muscles increase the angle between the joints.
C is wrong because these muscles move the body towards midline.
D is correct because abductor muscles are those muscles that move the body away from midline.

questions.explanation: Option D is the correct option because an abductor muscle is any of the muscles that cause movement of a limb away from the midplane of the body or away from a neighbouring part or limb. Option A is not correct because a flexor muscle is any of the muscles that decrease the angle between bones on two sides of a joint, as in bending the elbow or knee. Option B is not correct because an extensor muscle is any of the muscles that increase the angle between members of a limb, by straightening the elbow or knee. Option C is not correct because the adductor muscle, whose contraction moves a limb or other part of the body towards the midline of the body or another part.

questions.text: Muscles are composed of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A suggests that muscles are composed of silica. This statement is not correct. Silica is a chemical compound of silicon and oxygen, and it is not a major component of muscles. Muscles primarily consist of proteins, water, minerals, and other organic compounds, but not silica.

Option B suggests that muscles are composed of polyester threads. This statement is not correct. Polyester is a synthetic polymer and has no relation to the composition of muscles. Muscles are made up of specialized muscle fibers, connective tissues, blood vessels, and nerves.

Option C suggests that muscles are composed of groups of cell fibers. This statement is partly correct. Muscles are indeed composed of groups of muscle fibers. Muscle fibers are long, cylindrical cells that contain contractile proteins, actin, and myosin, responsible for muscle contraction. Multiple muscle fibers are bundled together to form muscle tissue, and different muscle tissues combine to make muscles.

Option D suggests that muscles are composed of calcium and phosphorus. This statement is not correct. While calcium and phosphorus are essential minerals for various biological processes in the body, they are not the primary components of muscles. As mentioned earlier, muscles are mainly composed of proteins (actin and myosin), water, and other organic compounds.

questions.explanation: Option (c) suggests that muscles are composed of groups of cell fibers. This statement is partly correct. Muscles are indeed composed of groups of muscle fibers. Muscle fibers are long, cylindrical cells that contain contractile proteins, actin, and myosin, responsible for muscle contraction. Multiple muscle fibers are bundled together to form muscle tissues, and different muscle tissues combine to make muscles.

questions.text: A muscle of fascicle is a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Connective tissue, specifically perimysium, surrounds and holds together the muscle fibres within a muscle fascicle, but it is not the primary component of a fascicle itself.

Myofibrils are structures found within muscle fibres, and they consist of contractile proteins responsible for muscle contraction. While myofibrils are present within muscle fibres, they are not the defining characteristic of a muscle fascicle.

It is taken directly from the definition of a muscle fascicle. A muscle fascicle is a bundle of muscle fibres (also known as muscle cells or muscle fibres) that are surrounded and held together by connective tissue called perimysium. Multiple fascicles together make up a muscle.

Muscle fibres are also referred to as muscle cells because they are elongated, multinucleated cells. However, the term "muscle cells" alone does not accurately describe a muscle fascicle, which is a specific organizational structure within a muscle.

questions.explanation: Correct option: C It is taken directly from the definition of muscle fascicle. A muscle fascicle is a bundle of muscle fibres (also known as muscle cells or muscle fibres) that are surrounded and held together by connective tissue called perimysium. Multiple fascicles together make up a muscle.

questions.text: Which of the following is known as a bony joint?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Synostosis is a type of bony joint, also known as a synostotic joint, where two bones have fused.
This option is incorrect. A symphysis is a type of cartilaginous joint where the bones are connected by fibrocartilage.
Bony joints are joints between two or more bones without intervening cartilage or fibrocartilage, other than articular cartilage.
This option is incorrect. The interosseous membrane is a sheet of fibrous tissue connecting bones, but not a joint itself.

questions.explanation: Option A: Synostosis is the union or fusion of adjacent bones by the growth of a bony substance, either as a normal process during growth or as the result of ankylosis. Option B: Symphysis is a type of joint where two bones are connected by fibrocartilage. Examples of symphysis joints include the pubic symphysis in the pelvis and the intervertebral discs between the vertebrae. Option C: Bony joints are joints between two or more bones without intervening cartilage or fibrocartilage, other than articular cartilage. Option D: The Interosseous membrane is not a type of joint. It is a broad, fibrous sheet of connective tissue that connects the shafts of two long bones, such as the radius and ulna in the forearm, and helps to stabilize them.

questions.text: Which of the following movements are possible in a pivot joint?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Flexion and extension are properties of hinge joints, so this option is incorrect.
Adduction and abduction are properties of ball-and-socket joints. So this option is also incorrect.
Rotation is performed by a pivot joint; hence, this option is the correct one.
Extension and flexion are properties of the hinge joint, whereas rotation is performed by the pivot joint. So this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: A pivot joint allows rotation around an axis, e.g., between the first and second neck vertebrae. It is the movement of the pivot joint that allows one to move their head back and forth.

questions.text: The fibrous connective tissue that attaches a muscle to a bone is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tendons are fibrous connective tissues that attach muscles to bones. They are strong and flexible, allowing them to transmit the force generated by muscles to the bones, enabling movement at the joints. Tendons play a crucial role in the musculoskeletal system by providing stability and facilitating movement.

Ligaments are also fibrous connective tissues, but they serve a different function. Ligaments connect bones to other bones at the joints and provide stability to the joints, preventing excessive movement and protecting them from injury.

Reticular tissue is a type of connective tissue found in various organs and tissues, such as the liver, spleen, and lymph nodes. It forms a supportive framework within these organs but does not attach muscles to bones.

Cartilage is a type of connective tissue that is softer and more flexible than bone. It is found in various parts of the body, such as the nose, ears, and joints. Cartilage does not attach muscles to bones; instead, it provides cushioning and support at the joints.

questions.explanation: Option (a) is correct. Tendons are fibrous connective tissues that attach muscles to bones. They are strong and flexible, allowing them to transmit the force generated by muscles to the bones, enabling movement at the joints. Tendons play a crucial role in the musculoskeletal system by providing stability and facilitating movement.

questions.text: The hinge joint and ball and socket joints are types of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Freely movable joints, also known as synovial joints, allow a wide range of motion and include ball-and-socket joints (e.g., shoulder, hip), hinge joints (e.g., knee, elbow), and pivot joints (e.g., neck).
Slightly movable joints, also known as amphiarthroses, permit limited movement and are typically connected by cartilage. Examples include the joints between vertebrae in the spine.
Immovable joints, also known as synarthroses, do not allow any significant movement. They are rigid and provide structural stability. Examples include sutures in the skull.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Option A is the correct option because the six types of freely movable joints include ball and socket, saddle, hinge, condyloid, pivot, and gliding. Most joints within the human body are of this type. Motion is the purpose of the joint. Option B is not correct because in 'slightly movable' joints, two or more bones are held together so tightly that only limited movement is permitted. For example, the vertebrae of the spine. Option C is not correct because in 'immovable' joints, the two or more bones are in close contact, but no movement can occur. For example, the bones of the skull. Option D is not correct because Option A is correct.

questions.text: Metacarpophalangeal joints are examples of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hinge joints are the joints that allow movement in two directions.

A saddle joint is a type of biaxial and movable joint that allows movements on two planes–flexion or extension and abduction or adduction. This joint is mainly formed between the bones, whose articulating surfaces have both concave and convex.

Condyloid joints, also known as ellipsoid joints, are composed of an egg-shaped bone known as a condyle that fits into a similarly shaped cavity. Although it sounds similar to a ball and socket joint, condyloid joints only allow for forward-backward and side-to-side movement and do not allow rotation.
Ball and socket joints are freely movable joints that allow movement in all directions.

questions.explanation: Metacarpophalangeal joints are the intersection of the metacarpals and the phalanges in the hand. These joints are condyloid. A condyloid joint is a type of synovial joint where the articular surface of one bone has an ovoid convexity sitting within an ellipsoidal cavity of the other bone. It allows movement in two planes (i.e., flexion or extension, abduction or adduction).

questions.text: Which of the following is not a unique feature of a synovial joint?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A joint capsule or articular capsule is an envelope surrounding a synovial joint.
Articular cartilage is the smooth, white tissue that covers the ends of bones where they come together to form joints and is present in synovial joints.

Synovial fluid is a viscous solution found in the cavities of synovial joints. The principal role of synovial fluid is to reduce friction between the articular cartilages of synovial joints during movement.

Fibrocartilage is the tough, very strong tissue found predominantly in the intervertebral disks and at the insertions of ligaments and tendons; it is similar to other fibrous tissues but contains cartilage ground substance and chondrocytes. "Fibrocartilage" is not present in all the synovial joints; therefore, it is not a unique characteristic of synovial joints.

questions.explanation: Fibrocartilage is the tough, very strong tissue found predominantly in the intervertebral disks and at the insertions of ligaments and tendons; it is similar to other fibrous tissues but contains cartilage ground substance and chondrocytes. "Fibrocartilage" is not present in all the synovial joints; therefore, it is not a unique characteristic of synovial joints.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of syndesmosis?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The radio-ulnar joints refer to the articulations between the radius and ulna bones in the forearm, allowing for rotational movements of the forearm.
Tibiofibular joints pertain to the connections between the tibia and fibula bones in the lower leg, contributing to stability during weight-bearing activities.

Syndesmoses are fibrous joints where two bones are connected by a ligament. Unlike most joints, the syndesmosis allows for very little movement between the bones. The most well-known syndesmosis joint in the body is the distal tibiofibular joint, where the tibia and fibula bones in the lower leg are connected by a syndesmosis. Other examples of syndesmosis include the joint between the radius and ulna bones in the forearm and the joint between the tarsal bones in the foot. Syndesmosis plays an important role in providing stability and support to the bones it connects.
Sutures are fibrous joints between bones in the skull, forming rigid connections that provide structural support and protect the brain.

questions.explanation: Syndesmoses are fibrous joints where two bones are connected by a ligament. Unlike most joints, the syndesmosis allows for very little movement between the bones. The most well-known syndesmosis joint in the body is the distal tibiofibular joint, where the tibia and fibula bones in the lower leg are connected by a syndesmosis. Other examples of syndesmosis include the joint between the radius and ulna bones in the forearm and the joint between the tarsal bones in the foot. Syndesmosis plays an important role in providing stability and support to the bones it connects.

questions.text: Joints are classified on the basis of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The amount of movement allowed by them
options.explanation: There are two ways to classify joints: based on their structure or based on their function. The structural classification divides joints into fibrous, cartilaginous, and synovial joints depending on the material composing the joint and the presence or absence of a cavity in the joint.

Joints are not classified based on the type of bones they join. Rather, joints are classified based on the range of motion they allow and the structure of the joint itself. The classification of joints is primarily based on their functional and structural characteristics.

Joints are indeed classified based on the nature of the structure they possess. The structural classification of joints is primarily based on the type of connective tissue that holds the bones together and the degree of movement allowed at the joint. There are two ways to classify joints: based on their structure or based on their function. The structural classification divides joints into fibrous, cartilaginous, and synovial joints depending on the material composing the joint and the presence or absence of a cavity in the joint.

options.text: Both Options A and C are correct
options.explanation: Joints are indeed classified based on the nature of the structure they possess. The structural classification of joints is primarily based on the type of connective tissue that holds the bones together and the degree of movement allowed at the joint.

questions.explanation: Joints are indeed classified based on the nature of the structure they possess. The structural classification of joints is primarily based on the type of connective tissue that holds the bones together and the degree of movement allowed at the joint. There are two ways to classify joints: based on their structure or based on their function. The structural classification divides joints into fibrous, cartilaginous, and synovial joints depending on the material composing the joint and the presence or absence of a cavity in the joint.

questions.text: Which of the following bone forms the hip joint with the hip bone?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The tibia is the larger of the two bones. It supports most of your weight and is an important part of both the knee joint and ankle joint.

The fibula is a long bone in the lower extremity that is positioned on the lateral side of the tibia. It is located just behind the tibial head at the knee joint and then runs down the lateral aspect of the leg until it reaches the ankle joint.
Tarsals are the bones that make up the ankle.
The femur is your thigh bone. The femur is the only bone located within the human thigh. It is both the longest and the strongest bone in the human body, extending from the hip to the knee.

questions.explanation: The hip joint is the junction where the hip joins the leg to the trunk of the body. It is comprised of two bones: the thighbone or femur, and the pelvis. OPTION A: The tibia is the larger of the two bones. It supports most of your weight and is an important part of both the knee joint and ankle joint. OPTION B: The fibula is a long bone in the lower extremity that is positioned on the lateral side of the tibia. It is located just behind the tibial head at the knee joint and then runs down the lateral aspect of the leg until it reaches the ankle joint. OPTION C: Tarsals are the bones that make up the ankle. OPTION D: The femur is your thigh bone. The femur is the only bone located within the human thigh. It is both the longest and the strongest bone in the human body, extending from the hip to the knee.

questions.text: What is the medical terminology for the pain in joints?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chondritis is the inflammation of cartilage.
Medical terminology for pain in joints is 'arthritis' or 'arthralgia'.
Synovitis occurs when the synovium of a joint becomes inflamed.
Rheumatic diseases are caused by the body's immune system attacking healthy body tissues, affecting joints, ligaments, tendons, and muscles.

questions.explanation: Option A: Chondritis is the inflammation of the cartilage. Option: B Medical terminology for pain in joints is 'arthritis' or 'arthralgia'. Option C: Synovitis occurs when the synovium of a joint becomes inflamed. Option D: Rheumatic diseases are caused by the body's immune system attacking healthy body tissues, affecting joints, ligaments, tendons, and muscles.

questions.text: Knee, elbow, ankle, and interphalangeal joints are examples of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. A saddle joint is a type of synovial joint where the articulating surfaces of the bones resemble the shape of a saddle. The bones in a saddle joint can move in two planes, allowing for a variety of movements. However, the knee, elbow, ankle, and interphalangeal joints do not have the characteristic shape and movement of a saddle joint.

This option is incorrect as well. A condyloid joint, also known as an ellipsoidal joint, is a type of synovial joint where the rounded surface of one bone fits into the concave surface of another bone. This type of joint allows for movement in two planes, similar to a saddle joint. However, the knee, elbow, ankle, and interphalangeal joints do not fit the description of a condyloid joint.

This option is also incorrect. A ball-and-socket joint is a type of synovial joint where the rounded end of one bone fits into the cup-shaped socket of another bone. This type of joint allows for a wide range of movements, including rotation. Examples of ball and Socket joints in the body include the hip joint and the shoulder joint. However, the knee, elbow, ankle, and interphalangeal joints do not possess the structure and movement characteristics of a ball and socket joint.

The correct answer is "hinge joint" because these joints predominantly allow movement in one plane of motion, similar to the opening and closing of a hinge. The knee joint allows flexion (bending) and extension (straightening), the elbow joint allows flexion and extension of the forearm, and the ankle joint allows dorsiflexion (lifting the foot upwards) and plantarflexion (pointing the foot downwards). Hinge joints are characterized by the presence of a convex surface fitting into a concave surface, enabling stable movement along a single axis.

questions.explanation: Hinge joints are a type of joint that functions much like the hinge on a door, allowing bones to move in one direction back and forth with limited motion along other planes.

questions.text: Hyaline cartilage forms a joint between a _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Growing bone refers to the process of bone development and formation, primarily occurring during childhood and adolescence. It involves the activity of specialized cells, such as osteoblasts (bone-forming cells) and osteoclasts (bone-resorbing cells), leading to the lengthening and strengthening of bones. This process is crucial for overall skeletal growth and development.

Mature bones refer to fully developed and hardened bones that have reached their optimal size, shape, and density. During bone maturation, the bone-forming activity of osteoblasts gradually balances with the bone-resorbing activity of osteoclasts, resulting in a stable bone structure. Mature bones provide structural support, protect internal organs, and serve as attachment points for muscles and ligaments.

Lamellar bone is a type of mature bone tissue characterized by its organized and layered structure. It consists of concentric layers of mineralized matrix called lamellae, which surround central channels called Haversian canals (or osteons in compact bone). Lamellar bone provides strength and resilience to the skeletal system and is a key component in the overall structure of mature bones.
The term "secondary bone" is not commonly used in anatomy or biology.

questions.explanation: Option A is the correct option because 'Hyaline cartilage' exists on the sternal ends of the ribs, in the larynx, trachea, and bronchi, and on the articulating surfaces of bones. It gives the structures a definite but pliable form. The presence of collagen fibres makes such structures and joints strong, but with limited mobility and flexibility. Throughout childhood and adolescence, there remains a thin plate of hyaline cartilage between the diaphysis and epiphysis known as the growth plate. Eventually, this hyaline cartilage will be removed and replaced by bone, so it forms a joint between growing bones.

questions.text: A type of joint found at the articulation between teeth and the sockets of the maxilla is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Syndesmosis refers to a type of joint in which two adjacent bones are connected by a ligament, allowing limited movement. Examples include the distal tibiofibular joint in the lower leg.

Sutures are fibrous joints in the skull where adjacent bones are tightly connected by dense fibrous tissue. They are immovable joints, contributing to the overall stability and protection of the brain.

Gomphosis is a type of fibrous joint where a cone-shaped peg fits into a socket, connecting a tooth to its socket in the jawbone. This joint provides stability and support but allows limited movement.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: A gomphosis is the specialised fibrous joint that anchors the root of a tooth into its bony socket within the maxillary bone (upper jaw) or mandible bone (lower jaw) of the skull. A gomphosis is also known as a peg-and-socket joint.

questions.text: Which of the following statement is NOT true?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Condyloid joints allow movement in two planes (biaxial), such as flexion-extension and abduction-adduction. They do not permit rotation, thus they are not triaxial. Examples include the radiocarpal joint in the wrist.
Synovial fluid is a viscous fluid found within synovial joints. Its primary role is to lubricate the joint, thereby reducing friction between the articulating surfaces during movement.

options.text: Coronal suture is for parietal and frontal bone articulation
options.explanation: The coronal suture is indeed the fibrous joint that connects the parietal bones to the frontal bone of the skull.
Synostosis refers to the fusion of two or more bones into a single bone, forming a bony joint. This process eliminates the joint, resulting in a rigid structure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option A: Condyloid joint is triaxial. Condyloid joints are biaxial, allowing movement in two planes but not rotation, which means they are not triaxial. Examples of condyloid joints include the wrist joint (radiocarpal joint). Option B is true because synovial fluid reduces friction in synovial joints. Option C is also true; the coronal suture connects the parietal and frontal bones. Finally, Option D is correct in stating that synostosis is a bony joint, as it involves the fusion of bones into a single unit.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a unique feature of a synovial joint?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A joint capsule or articular capsule is an envelope surrounding a synovial joint.
Articular cartilage is the smooth, white tissue that covers the ends of bones where they come together to form joints and is present in synovial joints.

Synovial fluid is a viscous solution found in the cavities of synovial joints. The principal role of synovial fluid is to reduce friction between the articular cartilages of synovial joints during movement.

Fibrocartilage is the tough, very strong tissue found predominantly in the intervertebral disks and at the insertions of ligaments and tendons; it is similar to other fibrous tissues but contains cartilage ground substance and chondrocytes. "Fibrocartilage" is not present in all the synovial joints; therefore, it is not a unique characteristic of synovial joints.

questions.explanation: Fibrocartilage is the tough, very strong tissue found predominantly in the intervertebral disks and at the insertions of ligaments and tendons; it is similar to other fibrous tissues but contains cartilage ground substance and chondrocytes. "Fibrocartilage" is not present in all the synovial joints; therefore, it is not a unique characteristic of synovial joints.

questions.text: Which of the following sections of a sarcomere does not shorten during contraction?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The I band is a segment of a muscle sarcomere that contains only thin actin filaments. It appears light under a microscope and represents the region where actin filaments do not overlap with thick myosin filaments.

The H zone is a region within a muscle sarcomere where only thick myosin filaments are present. It is located at the center of the sarcomere and appears darker under a microscope when viewed alongside the I band. The H zone shortens during muscle contraction.

During muscle contraction, the A band of a sarcomere does not shorten. The A band is a dark-staining band in the sarcomere, and it represents the region where thick myosin filaments are located. These filaments are responsible for the overlapping region with thin actin filaments during muscle contraction.
This option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Even though the 'I band' and the 'H zone' change in length, the 'A band' remains the same in length. 'A' is not the answer because the I band reduces in size. 'B' is not the answer because the H zone disappears during contraction.

questions.text: Many sarcomeres in series make up the length of a:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Microtubules are tubular structures within cells that provide structural support, serve as tracks for intracellular transport, and play a key role in cell division. They are composed of tubulin protein subunits and form part of the cytoskeleton.

Many sarcomeres in series make up the length of a myofibril. A sarcomere is the basic structural and functional unit of a muscle fiber (muscle cell). It is the region of a muscle fiber between two Z-lines (Z-discs), and it contains all the contractile elements responsible for muscle contraction. Sarcomeres are arranged in series along the length of myofibrils, which are long, cylindrical structures within muscle fibers. Multiple myofibrils run in parallel within a muscle fiber, and the contraction of sarcomeres in these myofibrils leads to the overall contraction of the muscle fiber. When many sarcomeres contract in series, the myofibrils shorten, causing the entire muscle fiber to contract. This contraction process is essential for muscle movement and function in the body.

Myosin filament is a protein structure in muscle cells that interacts with actin filaments during muscle contraction. It is a motor protein responsible for the sliding movement of filaments, leading to muscle contraction.

The M-line is a structural protein in the center of a muscle sarcomere that helps stabilize and organize the thick myosin filaments during muscle contraction. It contributes to the overall structure of the sarcomere.

questions.explanation: The subunits of a 'myofibril' are sarcomeres.

questions.text: Which of the following is the name of the modified endoplasmic reticulum found in muscle cells?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: T-tubules, or transverse tubules, are invaginations of the cell membrane in muscle cells. They play a crucial role in transmitting electrical impulses (action potentials) into the cell, ensuring synchronized muscle contraction.

A sarcomere is the basic structural unit of muscle fibers. It is the segment between two Z lines and consists of overlapping actin and myosin filaments. Contraction and relaxation of sarcomeres enable muscle movement.
There is no specific term "myoplasmic reticulum."

The modified endoplasmic reticulum found in muscle cells is called the "sarcoplasmic reticulum" (SR). The sarcoplasmic reticulum is a specialized type of endoplasmic reticulum (ER) that is specifically found in muscle cells, both in skeletal muscle and cardiac muscle. The primary function of the sarcoplasmic reticulum is to store and regulate calcium ions (Ca2+). Calcium plays a crucial role in muscle contraction. When a muscle is stimulated to contract, an action potential is generated, which leads to the release of calcium ions from the sarcoplasmic reticulum into the muscle cell's cytoplasm (sarcoplasm). The increased calcium concentration in the cytoplasm triggers a series of events that ultimately lead to muscle contraction.

questions.explanation: The sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) is a membrane-bound structure found within muscle cells that is similar to the smooth endoplasmic reticulum in other cells. The main function of the SR is to store calcium ions (Ca2+).

questions.text: When a muscle fibre shortens, the following also shortens:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An actin filament alone does not shorten during muscle contraction. Rather, the actin filaments slide over the myosin filaments, causing the sarcomere to shorten.

For a muscle cell to contract, the sarcomere must shorten. However, with thick and thin filaments, the components of sarcomeres do not shorten. The A band stays the same width and, at full contraction, the thin filaments overlap. When a sarcomere shortens, some regions shorten, whereas others stay the same length.

options.text: Both Options A and B are correct
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Just option B is correct.
Myosin filament alone does not shorten during muscle contraction. Rather, the myosin filaments slide over the actin filaments, causing the sarcomere to shorten.

questions.explanation: Option A: The Actin filament alone does not shorten during muscle contraction. Rather, the actin filaments slide over the myosin filaments, causing the sarcomere to shorten. Option B: For a muscle cell to contract, the sarcomere must shorten. However, with thick and thin filaments, the components of sarcomeres do not shorten. The A band stays the same width and, at full contraction, the thin filaments overlap. When a sarcomere shortens, some regions shorten, whereas others stay the same length. Option C: As explained above, this is an incorrect option. Option D: Myosin filament alone does not shorten during muscle contraction. Rather, the myosin filaments slide over the actin filaments, causing the sarcomere to shorten.

questions.text: Which of the following changes in length during sarcomere contraction?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thick filaments from the A band. They are surrounded by thin filaments on two sides linearly. When a muscle contracts, its length remains the same. Hence, this option is incorrect.
During muscle contraction, the thin filaments slide past the thick filaments or M-line.

The H-zone is the bright region in the middle of a sarcomere. It is because the edges of thick filaments they are overlapped with thin filaments, hence making the region crowded and appear dark under a microscope. However, in the middle, where only thick filaments are present, the region appears lighter and brighter. This region is called Hthe -zone. When the muscle contracts, thin filaments move towards the center of thick filaments, making the H-zone smaller. So, this option is correct.

options.text: All of these options are correct
options.explanation: As only option C is correct, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The H zone—the central region of the A zone—contains only thick filaments and is shortened during contraction. Remember that the actin and myosin filaments themselves do not change length, but instead slide past each other.

questions.text: Muscle contraction depends on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Without a nerve impulse, muscles do not contract on their own. Hence, it is a necessity, but it also needs energy and calcium. So this option is correct.
Without enough energy, muscles cannot contract, but this option cannot be marked correctly because options A and C are also correct.
The release of calcium ions is also a must for muscle contraction. Hence, this option is correct, along with options A and B. So we cannot mark this option correct.

options.text: All of these options are correct
options.explanation: As all options are correct, we will mark this option as the correct one.

questions.explanation: Muscle contraction begins when the nervous system generates a signal. The signal, an impulse called an action potential, travels through a type of nerve cell called a motor neuron. The source of energy that is used to power the movement of contraction in working muscles is adenosine triphosphate (ATP). Ca2+ ions play an important role in muscle contraction by creating interactions between the proteins, myosin, and actin. The Ca2+ ions bind to the C component of the actin filament, which exposes the binding site for the myosin head to bind to, to stimulate a muscle contraction.

questions.text: To produce ATP (adenosine triphosphate) in muscles, creatine phosphate (CP) transfers _ to adenosine diphosphate (ADP).
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Oxygen is not involved in the process; hence, the option is incorrect.
Adenosine is not involved in the process since it is already a part of ADP. Hence, the option is incorrect.
Cytosine is not involved in the process; hence, the option is incorrect
By adding a phosphate group, ADP is transformed into ATP. The phosphate group is transferred by creatine phosphate, which forms creatinine as a by-product. Hence, this option is the correct one.

questions.explanation: By adding a phosphate group, ADP is transformed into ATP. The phosphate group is transferred by creatine phosphate, which forms creatinine as a by-product. Hence, option D is the correct one.

questions.text: An entire skeletal muscle is surrounded by _?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cell membrane surrounding a skeletal muscle fibre is known as the sarcolemma.
The outermost sheath of connective tissue surrounding each muscle fibre is known as the epimysium.
Option A is not correct.
Microtubules are tiny, tube-shaped structures that help support and shape cells, and also help move chromosomes during cell division.

questions.explanation: The outermost sheath of connective tissue covering each muscle is called the epimysium. Each muscle is made up of groups of muscle fibers called 'fascicles' surrounded by a connective tissue layer called 'Perimysium'. So, option B is correct.

questions.text: A motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies is called a(n) _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A suggests that a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies are called a motor unit. This statement is correct. A motor unit consists of a motor neuron (nerve cell) and all the muscle fibers (muscle cells) it innervates or supplies. When the motor neuron is stimulated, all the muscle fibers in the motor unit contract simultaneously.

Option B suggests that a neuromuscular junction is the term for a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies. This statement is not correct. The neuromuscular junction is a specialized synapse between a motor neuron and a muscle fiber. It is the point where the motor neuron releases neurotransmitters (acetylcholine) to stimulate the muscle fiber to contract. It is a crucial structure for communication between the nervous system and muscles, but it does not encompass all the muscle fibers supplied by a single motor neuron.

Option C suggests that a neural unit is the term for a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies. This statement is not correct. The term "neural unit" is not a standard or widely used in biology or neuroscience.

Option D suggests that microtubules are the term for a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies. This statement is not correct. Microtubules are a component of the cytoskeleton of cells and are involved in various cellular processes, but they are not related to the concept of a motor neuron and the muscle fibers it innervates.

questions.explanation: A motor unit consists of a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies, so the correct answer is Option A. The neuromuscular junction is a synapse between the terminal motor neuron and the muscle fiber.

questions.text: Muscles are composed of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A suggests that muscles are composed of silica. This statement is not correct. Silica is a chemical compound of silicon and oxygen, and it is not a major component of muscles. Muscles primarily consist of proteins, water, minerals, and other organic compounds, but not silica.

Option B suggests that muscles are composed of polyester threads. This statement is not correct. Polyester is a synthetic polymer and has no relation to the composition of muscles. Muscles are made up of specialized muscle fibers, connective tissues, blood vessels, and nerves.

Option C suggests that muscles are composed of groups of cell fibers. This statement is partly correct. Muscles are indeed composed of groups of muscle fibers. Muscle fibers are long, cylindrical cells that contain contractile proteins, actin, and myosin, responsible for muscle contraction. Multiple muscle fibers are bundled together to form muscle tissue, and different muscle tissues combine to make muscles.

Option D suggests that muscles are composed of calcium and phosphorus. This statement is not correct. While calcium and phosphorus are essential minerals for various biological processes in the body, they are not the primary components of muscles. As mentioned earlier, muscles are mainly composed of proteins (actin and myosin), water, and other organic compounds.

questions.explanation: Option (c) suggests that muscles are composed of groups of cell fibers. This statement is partly correct. Muscles are indeed composed of groups of muscle fibers. Muscle fibers are long, cylindrical cells that contain contractile proteins, actin, and myosin, responsible for muscle contraction. Multiple muscle fibers are bundled together to form muscle tissues, and different muscle tissues combine to make muscles.

questions.text: Calcium, during muscle contraction, binds with?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because during muscle contraction, calcium does not bind with tropomyosin.
This option is correct because during muscle contraction, calcium binds with troponin C.
This option is incorrect because during muscle contraction, calcium does not bind with troponin I.
This option is incorrect because during muscle contraction, calcium does not bind with troponin T.

questions.explanation: The correct option is B. Calcium is released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum through calcium ion channels when the membrane of the T-tubular system is excited. It binds to Troponin C, causing it to conform, hence permitting the myosin head to latch onto the actin filament, initiating muscle contraction.

questions.text: The thick and thin filaments of myofibrils are arranged in units called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the Z-band (Z-line, Z-disc) defines the boundary of the sarcomere in striated muscle and bisects the I-band of neighboring sarcomeres.
This option is incorrect because actin is a family of globular multi-functional proteins that form microfilaments in the cytoskeleton, and the thin filaments in muscle fibrils.
This option is correct because a sarcomere is composed of two main protein filaments (thin actin and thick myosin filaments), which are the active structures responsible for muscular contraction.
This option is incorrect because sarcolemma, also called myolemma, is the cell membrane surrounding a skeletal muscle fiber or a cardiomyocyte.

questions.explanation: A sarcomere is the basic contractile unit of a myocyte (muscle fibre). A sarcomere is composed of two main protein filaments (thin actin and thick myosin filaments), which are the active structures responsible for muscular contraction.

questions.text: How many thin filaments are arrayed around each thick filament within a sarcomere?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect as well. The number of thin filaments surrounding each thick filament in a sarcomere is greater than four.
This option is incorrect as well. The number of thin filaments surrounding each thick filament in a sarcomere is greater than four.

Within a sarcomere, there are six thin filaments arranged around each thick filament. The thin filaments are composed of actin and are responsible for interacting with the thick filaments during muscle contraction.
This option is incorrect. The number of thin filaments surrounding each thick filament in a sarcomere is not eight.

questions.explanation: Option A: This option is incorrect as well. The number of thin filaments surrounding each thick filament in a sarcomere is greater than four. Option B: This option is incorrect as well. The number of thin filaments surrounding each thick filament in a sarcomere is greater than four. Option C: Within a sarcomere, there are six thin filaments arranged around each thick filament. The thin filaments are composed of actin and are responsible for interacting with the thick filaments during muscle contraction. Option D: This option is incorrect. The number of thin filaments surrounding each thick filament in a sarcomere is not eight.

questions.text: Which of the following steps occurs immediately after the binding of Ca2+ with a troponin molecule during muscle contraction?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Binding sites of actin get attached to the myosin head
options.explanation: The binding sites of actin get attached to the myosin head: This step occurs later in the muscle contraction process. Once the troponin-tropomyosin complex moves away from the binding sites on actin, the myosin heads of the thick filaments can attach to these exposed binding sites.

options.text: Troponin uncovers the actin binding sites
options.explanation: Troponin uncovers the actin binding sites: This option is incorrect. Troponin does not "uncover" the actin binding sites. Instead, troponin undergoes a conformational change upon binding with calcium ions, which causes the troponin-tropomyosin complex to move away from the myosin-binding sites on actin, allowing muscle contraction to occur.

options.text: Ca2+ goes back inside sarcoplasmic reticulum
options.explanation: Ca2+ goes back inside the sarcoplasmic reticulum: This step occurs after muscle contraction is complete. Once the muscle contraction signal is no longer needed, the calcium ions are actively pumped back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum, returning the calcium ion concentration to its resting level.

options.text: Tropomyosin gets removed from the binding sites of actin filaments
options.explanation: Soon after the binding of Calcium ions, tropomyosin is removed from the binding sites of actin filaments. It provides space for the myosin filament to attach to the actin filament for muscle contraction.

questions.explanation: Soon after the binding of Calcium ions, tropomyosin gets removed from the binding sites of actin filaments". It provides space for the myosin filament to attach to the actin filament for muscle contraction.

questions.text: The contractile protein of a muscle is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Actin is a thin filament protein that plays a crucial role in muscle contraction but is not the primary contractile protein responsible for generating force.
Myosin is the thick filament protein that interacts with actin to produce muscle contraction and has ATPase activity to provide the energy for this process.
Tubulin is a protein that forms microtubules and is involved in cellular structure and transport but is not directly involved in muscle contraction.
Tropomyosin is a regulatory protein that covers actin binding sites during muscle relaxation but does not perform contraction itself.

questions.explanation: Myosin interacts with actin filaments for contraction and has ATPase activity.

questions.text: The smallest contractile unit of muscle contraction, called a sarcomere, is the area between two?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The H zone is the lighter region in the middle of the A-band, where there are only myosin filaments. It shortens during muscle contraction as actin filaments slide toward the M line.
The M line is found in the center of the sarcomere and holds the thick myosin filaments together, but does not define the boundaries of a sarcomere.
The Z line, or Z-disc, defines the boundaries of a sarcomere. It is the site where actin filaments are anchored and marks the end of one sarcomere and the beginning of another.
'Z zone' is not a recognized term in muscle anatomy, making it an incorrect choice for this question.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the Z line. The sarcomere is the basic contractile unit of muscle fibers and is defined by the area between two Z lines. These lines serve as anchor points for actin filaments and help maintain the structure during contraction. The H zone and M line are internal components of the sarcomere that do not define its boundaries. The term 'Z zone' is not used in muscle anatomy; thus, it is not a correct option.

questions.text: _ is formed by two pairs of light chains, and one pair of heavy chains.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Actin is another protein that is essential for muscle contraction. It forms long, thin filaments that are arranged in parallel with the myosin filaments. When myosin binds to actin, it uses the energy from ATP to produce force, which causes the muscle to contract.

Myosin is a protein that plays a key role in muscle contraction. It is composed of two pairs of light chains and one pair of heavy chains. The light chains are smaller in size compared to the heavy chains. The combination of light chains and heavy chains forms the structure of myosin, which is responsible for the interaction between myosin and actin during muscle contraction.
Myofibrils are the basic unit of muscle tissue. They are composed of myosin and actin filaments, as well as other proteins that help to regulate muscle contraction.
Fibroin is a protein that is found in silk. It is composed of long, twisted chains of amino acids. Fibroin is not involved in muscle contraction.

questions.explanation: The answer is Myosin. Myosin is a protein that is essential for muscle contraction. It is composed of two pairs of light chains and one pair of heavy chains. The heavy chains form the core of the myosin molecule, and the light chains are involved in binding to actin and other proteins. The combination of light chains and heavy chains forms the structure of myosin, which is responsible for the interaction between myosin and actin during muscle contraction.

questions.text: Which of the following is true of troponin and tropomyosin?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Troponin binds to myosin and tropomyosin binds to actin
options.explanation: Troponin does not bind to myosin; thus, this option is incorrect.

options.text: Tropomyosin binds to actin and prevents the myosin head from binding to actin
options.explanation: In a relaxed muscle fibre, the binding sites for myosin cross-bridges on actin filaments are covered by tropomyosin. Troponin is a protein to which calcium ions bind during contraction. Tropomyosin binds to actin and prevents the myosin head from binding to actin; hence, this option is correct.

options.text: Both options A and B are correct
options.explanation: Option A is incorrect; thus, this option can't be selected.

options.text: None of these options are correct
options.explanation: Option B is correct; hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Option A: Troponin does not bind to myosin; thus, this option is incorrect. Option B: In a relaxed muscle fibre, the binding sites for myosin cross-bridges on actin filaments are covered by tropomyosin. Troponin is a protein to which calcium ions bind during contraction. Tropomyosin binds to actin and prevents the myosin head from binding to actin; hence, this option is correct. Option C: Option A is incorrect; thus, this option can't be selected. Option D: Option B is correct; hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.text: What is the specialized organelle in muscle cells that serves to sequester calcium?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: T-tubules are invaginations of the plasma membrane, which are present exclusively in striated muscle. T-tubules allow heart muscle cells to contract more forcefully by synchronizing calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum throughout the cell.

The sarcolemma, also called the myolemma, is the cell membrane surrounding a skeletal muscle fiber or a cardiomyocyte. It consists of a lipid bilayer and a thin outer coat of polysaccharide material (glycocalyx) that contacts the basement membrane.

A sarcomere is the basic contractile unit of a muscle fiber. Each sarcomere is composed of two main protein filaments—actin and myosin—which are the active structures responsible for muscular contraction. The most popular model that describes muscular contraction is called the sliding filament theory.

The sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) is a membrane-bound structure found within muscle cells that is similar to the smooth endoplasmic reticulum in other cells. The main function of the SR is to store calcium ions (Ca2+).

questions.explanation: Calcium diffusing in the cytoplasm between myosin and actin filaments of the muscle fibrils causes the filaments to slide into each other, triggering the contraction of the entire muscle fiber. The sarcoplasmic reticulum is a storage site for calcium within cells. Calcium is pumped into the sarcoplasmic reticulum by active transport and released in response to hormonal signals. A key to identifying the answer is to single out the sarcoplasmic reticulum as the only organelle in the given options.

questions.text: Which of the following molecules binds to troponin during muscle contraction, triggering tropomyosin to move away from the actin-binding sites and allowing the myosin head to form a cross-bridge?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is not correct. ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is a molecule that stores and releases energy within cells. It is often referred to as the "energy currency" of the cell, playing a crucial role in various cellular processes, including metabolism and cellular work.

This option is not correct. ADP (adenosine diphosphate) is a molecule formed when ATP (adenosine triphosphate) loses one of its phosphate groups through cellular processes that release energy. This conversion from ATP to ADP is a key step in energy transfer within cells.

This is the correct option. During muscle contraction, the calcium gates of the smooth endoplasmic reticulum open, releasing calcium into the cytosol. When a muscle is at rest, the tropomyosin is disposed in such a way that it covers the sites on the actin chain where the heads of myosin become attached. When calcium ions bind with the troponin molecule, they cause it to move slightly. This has the effect of displacing the tropomyosin and exposing the binding sites for the myosin head. Once the myosin head has become attached to the actin filament, ATP is hydrolysed and cross-bridges are broken down.

This option is not correct. Sodium is a chemical element with the symbol Na and atomic number 11. It is a highly reactive metal and an essential electrolyte in the body, playing a crucial role in various physiological functions, including nerve impulse transmission, muscle contraction, and fluid balance.

questions.explanation: During muscle contraction, the calcium gates of the smooth endoplasmic reticulum open, releasing calcium into the cytosol. When a muscle is at rest, the tropomyosin is disposed in such a way that it covers the sites on the actin chain where the heads of myosin become attached. When calcium ions bind with the troponin molecules, they cause them to move slightly. This has the effect of displacing the tropomyosin and exposing the binding sites for the myosin head. Once the myosin head has become attached to the actin filament, ATP is hydrolysed and cross-bridges are broken down.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is true concerning motor units?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Motor unit neurons only transmit one action potential during a contraction
options.explanation: A motor unit is made up of a motor neuron and all of the skeletal muscle fibers innervated by the neuron's axon terminals, including the neuromuscular junctions between the neuron and the fibers. Groups of motor units often work together as a motor pool to coordinate the contractions of a single muscle.

options.text: Large motor units are innervated first in a muscle, followed by smaller units in order to have a smooth contraction
options.explanation: Slow motor units, with higher post-synaptic motorneuron excitability, are activated first.

options.text: The neuron releases epinephrine across the synaptic cleft in order to stimulate an action potential in the sarcolemma
options.explanation: In vertebrates, motor neurons release acetylcholine (ACh), a small-molecule neurotransmitter, which diffuses across the synaptic cleft and binds to nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) on the cell membrane of the muscle fiber, also known as the sarcolemma.

options.text: Finger muscles are typically composed of smaller motor units
options.explanation: Smaller motor units are involved in many swift movements of our fingers. Hence, this option is correct.

questions.explanation: Option A: A motor unit is made up of a motor neuron and all of the skeletal muscle fibers innervated by the neuron's axon terminals, including the neuromuscular junctions between the neuron and the fibers. Groups of motor units often work together as a motor pool to coordinate the contractions of a single muscle. Option B: Slow motor units, with higher post-synaptic motor neuron excitability, are activated first. Option C: In vertebrates, motor neurons release acetylcholine (ACh), a small molecule neurotransmitter, which diffuses across the synaptic cleft and binds to nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs) on the cell membrane of the muscle fiber, also known as the sarcolemma. Option D: Smaller motor units are involved in many swift movements of our fingers.

questions.text: What is the role of the sarcoplasmic reticulum before a muscle contraction?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It actively pumps calcium ions into its lumen
options.explanation: It is not the role of the sarcoplasmic reticulum. So this is incorrect.

options.text: It releases calcium ions by active transport
options.explanation: It is not the role of the sarcoplasmic reticulum. So this is incorrect.

options.text: It creates the proteins needed to cover the actin filaments
options.explanation: It is not the role of the sarcoplasmic reticulum. So this is incorrect.

options.text: It releases calcium once an action potential reaches the sarcolemma.
options.explanation: The sarcoplasmic reticulum is a critical storage site for calcium ions, which it takes up from the cytoplasm. It also releases calcium ions when the muscle cell is triggered by nerve stimuli, resulting in muscle contraction.

questions.explanation: The sarcoplasmic reticulum is a critical storage site for calcium ions, which it takes up from the cytoplasm. It also releases calcium ions when the muscle cell is triggered by nerve stimuli, resulting in muscle contraction.

questions.text: The sarcoplasmic reticulum is a special type of endoplasmic reticulum. Based on this information, which of the following is associated with a sarcoplasmic reticulum?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) is a specialized network of tubules and sacs in muscle cells that are involved in the storage and release of calcium ions (Ca²+).

The sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) does contain Vesicles, but they are specialized membrane-bound structures called terminal cisternae that store and release Ca²+ during muscle contraction. These Vesicles are located at the ends of the SR tubules and are close to the T-tubules.

The sarcoplasmic reticulum is not associated with digestive enzymes. Digestive enzymes are typically found in the lysosomes of cells, which are membrane-bound organelles that contain hydrolytic enzymes for breaking down and recycling cellular waste.
The endoplasmic reticulum is made up of a network of tubules and also has vesicles that transport processed molecules to a target location.

questions.explanation: ER is made up of a network of tubules and also has vesicles that transport processed molecules to a target location.
